speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to tell me something that was important to you about the Lodz ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came to Lodz ghetto-- ghetto Lodz, I came with my father alone, without anything except what we had in our bag. We were assigned a room and we were able to get some bedding that remained after my father's brother, our uncle, that-- that passed away in the Lodz ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, I became the housekeeper. I worked all day as a foreman in this-- young-- you know, with young people. My father worked in the preparation of-- of production for the-- it was a central office of the factories-- the clothing factories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found myself cooking and washing the laundry. I remember I have learned-- I have seen at home that we were boiling the laundry, and washing the floor, and seeing everything should be clean. For my-- especially for my father. We-- the whole world looked up to my father. He was-- he was everything. Everyone had respect for my father because he was a mensch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Jewish it's said, to God and to light; to-- to God and to-- and to the individual person. If he-- he helped everyone, whoever he could. And this is the way-- there was nothing better in this world, in my eyes, anything more-- anything more important than my father. So here I am with this man alone that is constantly hungry, and I get these little rations here. And what do you do? What do-- What were the rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rations. We got a bread for eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big? Can you sh-- hold your hands high and show us how big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- this was the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Make the-- yes, so the camera can see it. That was for eight days?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- for eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: For two people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was for each.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So being we had two breads, we would cut up one bread in eight pieces, so we had the other one for the next four days. So I remember my father cutting the pieces and then this one had a little lump, so we had to straighten out the lump. So he'd get a piece for me and a piece for him. And no, it's not straight, it has another lump. And we kept smoothing out that piece of bread, too. By the time it was the ration for the next day, it was a quarter of what was intended to be. And so we were hungry the next day, and we kept straightening out these little pieces of bread. We used to get to our rations-- we would get at one time rad-- radishes. It's in Yiddish called [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Radish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Radish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made rice from that. I would cut it on a-- on a-- what is it called?

speaker: interviewer
text: Grater?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grater. Put it five times into hot water and then take a pinch of flour, burn the flour, and give it a little color with water and salt, and we were eating and it-- we filled up our stomachs. But from all these and I-- and my father kept saying, where did you learn to cook? I don't know. It just, you know, it comes to you. Necessity, I think, is the best teacher, it was the mother of invention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perhaps you had watched-- maybe you'd watched your mother when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Like, my father wouldn't let me into the factory, not to become a tailor. I certainly couldn't be in the kitchen-- no one could be in the kitchen when mother-- when mother cooked. But one thing in particular what I remember, the rations were getting smaller. My father was-- when we went into the ghetto, he was on the heavy side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every year, he would go to a spa. He had a problem with his stomach. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this year-- that year in the ghetto, after being there a couple of months, he says, my child, what is it with me? I feel so good. You know, my-- my stomach doesn't hurt me. What happened back home, Friday night, we took the fat-- my mother would take the fat from the chicken soup and serve it for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: All this fat stuff was laying on his stomach and here all of a sudden, he didn't have that. So he felt good for two, three months. Then the hunger set in and he started to get these cysts-- these abscess on his neck all around. And puss would come out and here I was to help. I put compresses and everything else, and I kept putting it and covering with-- with bandages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, the doctor, you know, the first aid where we had there, he said, nothing is going to help. His body needs fat. So where am I going to get fat? All I could is compensate in the rations where we had to make it a little better for my father. So one day, for dinner, we had five potatoes. That was for that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I washed the potatoes, you know, and I cooked them, and I put three potatoes in my father's bowl of soup. I would cook two liter of soup with five potatoes because-- to fill up the stomach. As I got up to bring something to the table-- I don't remember, salt or whatever it was-- I see there's a third potato in my plate-- in my bowl. I screamed at my father like never before. I wanted that potato so badly, but yet I felt the respect for my father, that he has to have it and it was going back and forth, and back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you split it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It had to be respect for my father. And maybe he split it, I don't remember. But I remember telling the story to my son. That's the only story that I ever told my oldest son-- or my youngest son-- what we went through in-- in the ghetto. And I think he was so affected by this that in his speech for U-- UJA breakfast-- or it was maybe [NON-ENGLISH] that he told this-- this-- this story. And this is the-- this is the way-- there were situations where the hunger had caused some people-- friends, relatives-- to steal from each other. Here, I'm not trying to make myself a saint, but my father did the same thing for me. We got a little bit of sugar, as I-- I noticed that my-- my portion was always a little more. He took from him and gave it to me. And this was going on all along the time that we were together in the ghetto. And towards the end when he-- when he saved my life, and he gave his life for it. So I don't know of any greater saint, of any finer person, of any dedicated father than my father was. And I will never forgive the world for letting my children grow up without knowing a grandfather or a grandmother. This is what I carry in my heart. That anger against those that didn't do anything. My father died three days after liberation. Three days. Three days. And those three days would have been shortened.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a fact. The world knows. Everyone knows. I just watched a program with America-- America's reaction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What is it actually called? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] America's behavior or whatever, and those-- that war could have been shortened by those two days, three days, and my father would have been alive and my children would have had a grandfather. And that I will never forgive all those responsible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't care-- I'm not so much worried about the revisionists today as I was-- when I mentioned to you I wrote this article or everyone gets so worked up a-- about it. Places like [GERMAN] are still the way they were during the Germans, in the German times. The Russians had liberated a camp and it stays full, com-- complete, exactly as it was during the German-- German's time. Other concentration camps are there intact. Auschwitz is there. Dachau is there. There are so many. There are so many. And now, when I hear to the programs, when I see what could have been done-- not to avoid, but to help save my family, our people. The world didn't care, and that-- that hurts me just as bad [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as what the-- what the Germans did. The rest of the world not doing anything, practically not doing anything, with very few exceptions. Very few exceptions. I didn't mean to get away on a political speech but this has nothing to do with poli--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It has to do with my pain all these years, especially when it came before a holiday. My chil-- our children never knew what it meant to have--

speaker: interviewer
text: Grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandparents. For that matter, relatives. Relatives. Close relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how they Lodz ghetto was ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as the-- it seems that when the-- the German-- the Russian front was getting close-- the Russians were getting closer and closer to Poland-- I mean, into Poland, to-- to Lodz, the ghetto, they decided to liquidate the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just the other day I read-- I happened to have here the prosecuting papers against-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mentioned the man's name before. The one that-- that sent us right and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Biebow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Biebow, right. Because the same Biebow, what I didn't-- forgot to tell you before, is when we were sent away-- those that were able to work got sent to the Lodz ghetto-- he stood on a pile of stones and talking to those healthier ones. Being probably afraid we shouldn't rise up or do anything or kill anyone, he gave a speech in German that we're going now to a place where we'll work and we will have Apfel und Apfelsine, which means apples and oranges. This is how good we're going to have it there, and if we work, we will be rewarded very well for our-- for our-- four our work. This same man-- I just, you know, read this-- I happened to have this-- very few people have this prosecuting-- the documents-- that he was sentenced to death and he was executed in-- in Poland. The plans, how he was-- wanted to extend the Lodz ghetto to use the Jewish people to the point as much as physically as they can give for the German effort and then destroy them. But this, like I said, the Russians-- they felt that the Russians are getting close to the Lodz ghetto. They-- they started to deport people to Auschwitz. First, they had offered bread for everyone will come. I think it was the street called [POLISH] where everyone will report and he will get the bread and jam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I and my father, we hid in a little space for a few days. How long can you hide without food? And on top of everything else, in that building, where we found empty and we hid in that-- in that crawling space into a wall, downstairs was a bakery and was driving us crazy. Crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, we decided we've got to give up. Because there was no future. No one, you know-- there were 800 people left in Lodz ghetto when the Russians came in, that hid out. We never thought that anyone could hide. We never knew that the Russians are so close or whatever. We just knew they'd decided to take the next step to our extermination. And then we came. They took us to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to this mark-- meeting place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the meeting place where we got this bread?

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you three days late because you hid for three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they had a certain time that people could report to them. There was no punishment because we just reported and nothing happened. We got the bread. We ate as much as we could on that-- the boxcar, cattle car, what they put us in. And you know, it's a strange thing. Once you're not hungry, you forget how precious that bread is. So I put it next to me and I fell asleep. When I woke up, of course, it was gone. But by the time we came to Birkenau, no one even thought of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you-- how many days were you in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you standing? Was there room to sit?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was room to sit. Yes, there was room to sit because, from that ghe-- from the ghetto, they tried to bring the people yet in a way not to scare us. Because the ghetto had [NON-ENGLISH] and other people that worked officially for the government, they were even sent in other cars-- in the passenger cars. But that-- that was very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your cattle train, were there men and women in your truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just men. I think it was just men. And we came to-- to Birkenau, and again the sele-- the selection started over. You know, they were sending people to the right, to the left. Didn't know what it meant. Didn't know what it meant till we heard from people that yelled over the barbed wire. You know, they had relatives that are waiting for them, people that were working by the crematorium, people that were working for the [GERMAN] commander. They told them what was happening and, you know, and they told them what to do and what not to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not to pre-- not to say that they're sick, not to say that they haven't got the strength, you know, always to be with the-- with the stronger people, things like that. But we were only there a short time-- I don't remember, eight or 10 days or something like that-- and we were sent to Kaufering in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go to that, tell me-- in those eight days, tell me what they did when you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first when we arrived, they were standing there with the dogs, with the whips. You know, who couldn't run away fast enough was-- was beaten with that. They were-- they were letting the dogs-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bite?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get at some-- bite at some people. But we were so occupied with getting into the-- into that line and into the middle-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] exactly, you know, I really, truly don't recall--

speaker: interviewer
text: Moving in there and having the showers? You don't remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we didn't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember doing that. But yet, we wound up-- we had to be doing it because we wound up with these striped uniforms-- you know, these concentration camps. So we must-- yes, we were shaven, of course. We came in and we were shaven all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: People, you know. It must've been other prisoners. They were definitely other prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it painful?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were shaving under the arm and all over. They were pulling, you know. But you were so concentrating of staying alive. You know, we're so concentrating on staying alive and kept looking for where my father is, con-- constantly. So we-- we had this-- this shower. They put some powder-- the DD-- what is that? The TT--

speaker: interviewer
text: Disinfectant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disinfectant, but there's a certain name. Anyway, they put this-- threw this powder on us-- you know, in the eyes and on the head and all over. And when we came out, we were given those stripes. And I remember distinctly that the man that was giving out the soup was a prisoner. He told my father he'll give him a little more soup if he'll change that suit for him because it was a little heavier. So of course, he changed it. Took it away from him. But it was so traumatic getting into that Birkenau and the kind of stories that we heard, that from here people go to work and everything will be OK. If-- if my father knew about it, I don't know. I didn't know about the chimneys. I didn't know-- I didn't know these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. Did you smell anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. I was so absorbed. You know, I have seen the chimneys. I have seen it after. But during-- during that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my mind was completely so absorbed about the-- the tragedy that has happened step after step. Here we lost the mother and the brother, now I'm concerned about where is my father?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is going to happen to him? He's getting older, he's had all these-- these cysts on his-- on his neck and he wasn't getting better. And here I saw they were sending people-- this much we understood. When we saw that they were taking only healthy people one way, we knew that the others are not going to a good place, but we didn't know exactly what. You know, we heard the rumors that no one would believe, but there wasn't talk yet. At that time, I didn't hear anything about-- about chimneys, you know? We heard about it and saw it later, you know? So now you're in there and you've got the uniform on, and then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just made us take little stones from here and put them there. They kept us busy for-- for at least eight days. And they-- if you didn't move fast enough, they would beat you. And mostly that would beat us is the people in the striped-- in the striped suits, those kapos, those people that were the-- the hardened-- they were criminals. Or-- or maybe some people just that wanted to survive. They kept-- they kept beating us for the, you know, to show for the Germans that were watching them with the dogs that they are the-- working for them. But these people must have thought that they will be the ones to survive. As a matter of fact, everyone thought they would be the one to survive. No one thought that he's going to die the next day, except those that threw themselves at the barbed wire. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this-- being in Birkenau was so fast, you know, that before we knew, we were taken on the railroad cars and we were sent to a division of Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaufering?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sent to Kaufering where there were-- Kaufering was a division of Dachau. There was-- there were 11 camps over there from Lager number einz, camp number-- number one, and camp 11. We arrived at four-- camp number four, which was six kilometers from the city of Landsberg am Lech. This was the city where Hitler wrote-- was imprisoned and wrote It's also the city where we lived after the war, before we came to the United States. And in that camp, they started to organize groups to go out to work. First, we cleaned up the camp and we prepared it for people, you know, other arrivals. We were the first ones. We had-- and then we started to go-- to go out to work. We worked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the fields. We worked laying cable. I don't know if it was electric cable-- probably telephone cables. There was a town nearby, Lechfeld, which is also very close-- it's on-- it's between Landsberg and Augsberg, where we laid the cables. It had to be telephone cables. And in between that, there were-- were woods where, after a while when people started to die, this is where they were very buried, in those woods between Lechfeld and Lands-- and Landsberg. And in the camp, it didn't take very long. We had first-- and there was one time, for a long time, that I was taken to work at night for a company called Firme Moll. Now this had to do with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just repeat that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moll, M-O-L-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Firme Moll.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are out-- they're out of Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-O-L-L-E?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-L, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moll, just with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Moll, yeah. I think they're still in business today. So we went deep underground. I think they were-- had to do something with the fire, too, with the rockets because it was a very strong construction of steel wires and cement, and my work-- my job was to carry sacks of cement, open them up, and pour it into [INAUDIBLE] big container. And doing that all night, that-- the dust from the-- from the cement went into my eyes. I had a mark for a long time-- a hole, literally-- in my shoulder. And the-- and the sacks, where we emptied, I would put around my legs and my body to keep me war-- to keep me warm. And I remember, the one time I worked with my fa-- with my father together. Somehow we managed always to work in the same place. We were lucky with this. And one night, there was a Polish loco-- locomotive driver that kept bringing raw materials, the cement. And my father recognized him-- someone that he was in the army with-- and he gave him a few [INAUDIBLE] boiled potatoes. It was like giving an injection of life-- a gift of life at that time. But soon what happened is that most of the people started to get sick in the camp. And as the dead were carried out, they kept bringing in more and more new people from other camps, or newer-- from other countries. There were-- I think there were a lot of French people there in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, that camp became a Krankenlager, a camp especially for sick. No one would go out except it was called the latrine commander, which it's the, like, cess-- they would take out the cesspools to the-- to wherever they went. And what was the other one? Oh, and the dead-- the dead [INAUDIBLE]. People that would take the dead people to bury them a few kilometers away from the-- from the camp. And most of the people had typhus. [INAUDIBLE] What is it called? Typhus that you get from lice. In German it's called [GERMAN]. I think it's just typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhus? It was a different kind of typhus. My father had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typhoid, right. Typhoid. With all these languages I get a little confused. Typhoid. And my father had it. Now, before all this happened, I was picked to go with the dead-- with the-- to carry out the dead people. I fell down on my knees to the Lagerfuhrer, which was, itself, [GERMAN]. He was also a prisoner but people said he wasn't the worst of people, so I kneeled-- I bent down on my knees and I says, please, have mercy. I have never handled dead people. Give me any kind of work. For some reason, he picked-- he said, go to this bunker and work over there. And he sent me to chop on roots, you know, the roots from wood. Trees were brought to this place and I chopped it up and they kept themselves warm in there with these these kapos. Or they had this special, you know, these Lageralteste with his buddies. They lived in a special barrack, so they used that wood, what I chopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a way, that was the only time-- it lasted about four weeks-- where I had enough to eat and I was-- felt real good about myself. I says, gee, I'm going to overcome this. So when you feel good, you think about your father. So I would bring in some food by the end of the day for my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time-- second time, third time, one of the time I came in-- I says-- I says, Dad, maybe I should stay here with you overnight. You know, you shouldn't be alone. My father didn't say anything. I stayed overnight. I never got up in the morning. I never heard of a-- of a sickness to catch you so suddenly. It must've been that there was-- these lice that were crawling all over there, must've been on me for some time that I got the sickness. I never got up from there till the end of-- till we were deported. We were taken out from this Lager Four till in April, when we-- when this camp was liquidated and we were sent to Lager One for the deportation-- for-- for transportation from there to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical attention at all for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called their medical-- what do they call it? Lavier. Lavier? I don't remember what it was called, really. Revier. Revier. That's what it is. Krankenrevier. But it was really nothing. In fact, they couldn't have given you-- not even aspirin. And they could give you compresses or tell you what it is, or try to help you in any primitive way that he could. But there were no medications.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never went in there to that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, people tried to stay away because those were the first ones that were taken out to make room for others. You tried to stay away. People didn't go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were moved to Kaufering Lager One?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lager One, yes. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how far between the two? Oh, just six kilometers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about six kilo-- it's the same-- the same village.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to walk, of course, it was-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we walked and we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. No. We-- I remember now. We were shoved into railroad cars. I remember that I had somewhere got hold of a-- of a raw potato and I ate it quickly. And I was so sick that I was sure going to die there. And I don't know how long we traveled but it couldn't be for too long. That was like in the afternoon and the six kilometers, we arrived in the evening. And in the evening, we were in-- they put-- shoved us into those barracks, the same kind as in-- in Lager Vier.

speaker: interviewer
text: You father was with you through this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my father. But, you know, skin and bone. I couldn't work anymore because I was grown to-- h here I had sores in the-- in the elbow, by the-- the arms. And I had sores over here, and I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just say-- the camera can't see that. Behind your legs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind my legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my arms, and I was kind of-- like-- like in a fetus type position. So we heard shots-- rifle shots. Not machine guns, not tanks, but rifle shots. And all we heard yelling is 'Alles raus, alles raus.' And I said to my father, I'm not moving. I think we should stay here. I can't no more. He says, my child, he says, we-- we're going to-- the war is coming to an end. This must be the end. Let's go. Let's just run. I said, we-- I can't. So I started walking with my father. And of course, going like-- you know, in a position the way I was. So this Stormfuhrer [GERMAN] had kicked me in my behind. The same man was caught and sentenced to death and he was executed. And when my father saw that-- saw that he picked me up-- God as my witness, I don't know how, with what strength he did. But he carried me to the train and-- that was standing not far away and put me on the-- on that railroad car.
